sentences.base_language: My friend looks a bit sad today.
sentences.target_language: 我的朋友今天看起来有点难过。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ de péngyǒu jīntiān kàn qǐlái yǒudiǎn nánguò.
sentences.grammarNote:
**看起来** (kàn qǐlái) = 'looks like / appears to'. This verb + **起来** pattern describes sensory impressions: **看起来** (looks), **听起来** (sounds), **闻起来** (smells), **吃起来** (tastes).
**有点** before an adjective = 'a little / somewhat' (with a slightly negative tone): **有点难过** (a little sad), **有点奇怪** (a little strange).
**难过** (nánguò) = sad/upset — **难** (difficult) + **过** (pass/go through) → hard to get through → sad. A vivid compositional meaning.

sentences.romanized: Wǒ wèn tā fāshēng le shénme shì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**发生了什么** = 'what happened'. **了** after the verb marks that the event occurred.
Embedded questions in Chinese don't change word order: **我问他发生了什么** (I ask him what happened) — no inversion needed.
**什么事** (shénme shì) = what matter/thing. **事** (shì) = matter/affair/thing — very common in phrases: **没事** (it's nothing), **出事了** (something happened / there's been an incident).

sentences.base_language: He says he had an argument with his girlfriend.
sentences.target_language: 他说他跟女朋友吵架了。
sentences.romanized: Tā shuō tā gēn nǚpéngyǒu chǎojià le.
sentences.grammarNote:
**跟** (gēn) = 'with (a person)'. Used for doing something together with or against someone: **跟朋友聊天** (chat with a friend), **跟他吵架** (argue with him).
**吵架** (chǎojià) = to have an argument / quarrel. **吵** = noisy/to quarrel, **架** = a fight/frame. **吵架了** = 'had an argument' (the **了** marks it as completed).
**吵架了** — when **了** comes at the very end, it can also signal that an unfortunate new situation has occurred.

**听到** = 'hear (and receive the info)' — **到** is a resultative complement showing the action succeeded. Compare: **听** (trying to hear) vs **听到** (actually heard it).
**遗憾** (yíhàn) = regret / sorry to hear — used for sympathy as well as personal regret. More formal and deeper than a simple 'sorry'.
Note the topic-comment structure: **听到这个** (hearing this) comes first as context, then the comment: **我很遗憾**.

sentences.base_language: I tell him it will be okay.
sentences.target_language: 我告诉他一切都会好的。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ gàosù tā yīqiè dōu huì hǎo de.
sentences.grammarNote:
**一切** (yīqiè) = 'everything / all'. When used with **都**, it emphasizes totality: **一切都会好** = 'everything will be okay'.
**会…的** is a confident prediction pattern — the sentence-final **的** adds reassurance and warmth.
**告诉** (gàosù) = to tell (someone something). Structure: **告诉 + person + [content]**: 我告诉他我很好 (I told him I was fine).

**互相** (hùxiāng) = 'mutually / each other'. It comes before the verb: **互相帮助** (help each other), **互相理解** (understand each other). Chinese uses this adverb where English uses 'each other.'
**支持** (zhīchí) = to support. **支** = to prop up/branch, **持** = to hold/maintain.
This is a topicless predicate sentence — '**Good friends** [as for them] support each other.' Very natural in Chinese.

sentences.base_language: He feels a little better after talking.
sentences.target_language: 聊完后他感觉好一些了。
sentences.romanized: Liáo wán hòu tā gǎnjué hǎo yīxiē le.
sentences.grammarNote:
**好一些** = 'a bit better'. **一些** (yīxiē) after an adjective = 'a bit / somewhat': **好一些** (a bit better), **快一些** (a bit faster).
The sentence-final **了** marks the change: he has now become somewhat better.
**聊完** = finished chatting — **聊** (liáo) = to chat, **完** = finished (resultative complement).

**就是** (jiùshì) = 'is exactly / that is precisely'. The **就** adds emphasis: 'That is EXACTLY the meaning of friendship.'
**意义** (yìyì) = meaning / significance. **意** = meaning/intention, **义** = meaning/righteousness.
An alternative more colloquial phrasing: **这才是朋友嘛** (That's what a real friend does!) — **才** emphasizes 'this and only this.'
